title: IRS Code 531 Meaning (Bankruptcy Dismissal) – For Tax Professionals
description: IRS Code 531 reflects bankruptcy dismissal activity. Learn what it usually means for collection status, what may resume, and what to document for next-step planning.
content:
IRS Transcript Codes • Built for 750,000+ U.S. Tax Professionals

IRS Code 531 Meaning

IRS Code 531 typically means the bankruptcy case was dismissed. This page explains when it shows up on transcripts, what it usually signals for collection status, and practical next steps. Want to see what clients get? Open the sample report, then run your own transcript when you’re ready.

Try the parser (requires credits)

What is IRS Code 531?

Plain-English definition: IRS Code 531 indicates bankruptcy dismissal activity was posted to the tax module. In most cases, it means the bankruptcy case ended without a discharge order eliminating eligible debts.

Common transcript type
Account Transcript
Usually appears near
Codes 520, 530, 532
Typical context
Bankruptcy timeline and collection posture changes
Client-friendly summary
"Your bankruptcy case was dismissed"

When does Code 531 appear?

  • After dismissal is entered: Posts after the IRS receives and processes the court dismissal information for the module.
  • Module-by-module: It can appear on specific tax periods and not others, depending on what was part of the bankruptcy case.
  • In a bankruptcy sequence: Commonly appears after Code 520 (filed) and around other case-status codes like 532 (case closed).

What does Code 531 usually signal?

Dismissal vs discharge

Dismissal means the case ended without a discharge order wiping out eligible debts. A discharge (often associated with discharge-related activity such as Code 530) is a different outcome and should be verified using court documents and transcript timing.

Collection posture may change

Because dismissal can end the automatic stay, IRS collection activity may resume. Confirm whether any other holds, enforcement limits, or representation actions apply before advising the client.

What should a tax professional do next?

  1. Confirm context: Review the dates and nearby bankruptcy codes to understand the sequence (520 → 531 → 532) and whether discharge activity (530) is present.
  2. Validate with documents: Match the transcript to court records (dismissal order, docket, affected periods) before concluding what protections ended.
  3. Document next steps: Capture current balance and posture, then outline next-step planning (compliance, payment options, collection holds, or fresh strategy).

Example: how this looks in a parsed report

Below is a simplified view of how Code 531 can appear in a client-ready transcript report with context:

Bankruptcy timeline
Code 520 – Bankruptcy or other legal action filed
Date: 03/22/2024
Code 531 – Bankruptcy case dismissed
Date: 06/18/2024
Code 532 – Bankruptcy case closed
Date: 06/18/2024

FAQ

What does IRS Code 531 mean on a transcript?

IRS Code 531 indicates bankruptcy dismissal activity posted to the tax module. It generally means the bankruptcy case was dismissed rather than discharged.

Does Code 531 mean collections can resume?

Often, yes. Dismissal can end the automatic stay and the IRS may resume collection activity. Confirm the transcript sequence and current account posture before advising the client.

How do I explain dismissal vs discharge to a client?

Dismissal means the case ended without a discharge order eliminating eligible debts. Discharge means the court entered an order that can eliminate certain debts, subject to exceptions. Use court documents plus the transcript timeline to explain what changed.

What other transcript codes should I check after Code 531 posts?

Check nearby bankruptcy timeline entries (520 and 532) and whether any discharge-related activity (530) posted. Then document current balance and collection posture for next-step planning.
